coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphdiyne (N-GDY) nanostructures are attracting increasing attention in electrochemical sensing field owing to its peculiar chemical structure, high structure defects, good electrical conductivity, favorable biocompatibility and hydrophilicity. A highly anti-interference electrocatalytic sensor based on N-GDY for the detection of dopamine (DA), a crucial neurotransmitter and chemical substance for numerous physiological processes, was demonstrated. The highly anti-interference ability was endowed by the abundant existing sp-N interactive sites on synthesized N-GDY surface that not only offered the very high-efficiency interactions between target DA molecules and developed interface but also effectively excluded other interferential component combinations which further powerfully boosted the high selectivity of fabricated sensor. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to explore, verify and reveal both the best-matched sp-N interactive sites for DA molecules on N-GDY surface and the correlative mechanism for electrocatalytic process towards DA. Electrocatalytic sensitivity was also enhanced due to the large electroactive surface area, more active sites, superior conductivity, and improved interfacial charge transfer of as-prepared N-GDY nanostructure. Such developed sensor achieved highly selective and sensitive DA detection with a very wide linear ranging from 1 to 550 µM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.46 µM, even in the real complex biofluids, where a good deal of biological components are co-existing. The superior performance towards target was principally attributed to its outstanding anti-interference ability and the analysis results in biofluids markedly declared its powerful potential for practical applications of such DA sensor.

head.ce:title: Highly anti-interference electrocatalytic sensing for dopamine with nitrogen-doped graphdiyne directly in biofluids

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphdiyne (N-GDY) nanostructures are attracting increasing attention in electrochemical sensing field owing to its peculiar chemical structure, high structure defects, good electrical conductivity, favorable biocompatibility and hydrophilicity. A highly anti-interference electrocatalytic sensor based on N-GDY for the detection of dopamine (DA), a crucial neurotransmitter and chemical substance for numerous physiological processes, was demonstrated. The highly anti-interference ability was endowed by the abundant existing sp-N interactive sites on synthesized N-GDY surface that not only offered the very high-efficiency interactions between target DA molecules and developed interface but also effectively excluded other interferential component combinations which further powerfully boosted the high selectivity of fabricated sensor. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were used to explore, verify and reveal both the best-matched sp-N interactive sites for DA molecules on N-GDY surface and the correlative mechanism for electrocatalytic process towards DA. Electrocatalytic sensitivity was also enhanced due to the large electroactive surface area, more active sites, superior conductivity, and improved interfacial charge transfer of as-prepared N-GDY nanostructure. Such developed sensor achieved highly selective and sensitive DA detection with a very wide linear ranging from 1 to 550 µM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.46 µM, even in the real complex biofluids, where a good deal of biological components are co-existing. The superior performance towards target was principally attributed to its outstanding anti-interference ability and the analysis results in biofluids markedly declared its powerful potential for practical applications of such DA sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For human, predominantly neurological diseases are closely associated with neurotransmitters, which are adopted by nervous system to transmit messages between neurons [1–3]. Dopamine (DA), a crucial neurotransmitter and chemical messenger, performs a profound significance in a large number of physiological processes such as neural signaling transduction, reward process in the brain and vasodilation outside the brain, and exerts a non-negligible effect on human behavior and physical functions such as motivation, movement and heart rate [4–7]. Abnormal DA levels in human blood, unfortunately, can be indicative of severe neurodegenerative disorders, for example, Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s diseases, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder and schizophrenia [8–10]. DA is considered as a very important biomarker for particular cancer strains like paraganglioma [10]. Therefore, there is a considerable challenging technological demand for a sensitive, selective, simple, and affordable platform to monitor low DA level in complex biofluids, because of its fairly low concentration of 195.8 × 10-12 M and 274.0 × 10-9 M, respectively, in human blood and urine [10]. To date, determination of DA can be carried out with multiple methods such as mass spectrometry, liquid chromatography, and bio-electronic technique. Because DA itself shows a strong redox property, it is capable of electrochemical or electrocatalytic sensing detections with readily signal output rather than using any enzyme or reporter or probes. As DA sensors, various bio-electronic techniques, including field-effect transistors, electrochemiluminescence and electrochemical methods, which can realize rapid response and miniaturized integration, have been largely investigated and demonstrated [11–15]. Among these detection strategies, their analytical sensitivity and selectivity towards DA are found to be two far-reaching limitation factors and two severe challenges, especially when these devices are exposed in biological media where a good deal of inherent interfering biomolecules are co-existing however DA itself is kept at an extremely low level [5]. Nanomaterials, especially accompanied with highly specific target interactive sites, with their enhanced sensing performance hold powerful promise to improve selectivity and sensitivity, and further to accelerate practical application. With distinct physical and chemical characteristic, large electroactive surface area, high conductivity and electrical mobility, nanomaterials have been widely explored for modifying electrodes to enhance performance of designed sensing platforms [5,9,11,15]. For example, nanostructured gold, such as gold nanoparticles and gold nanoclusters, showing exceptional conductivity and electrocatalytic activity are becoming ideal sensing materials [16]. Conductive polymers and metal oxides are also well-known as promising candidates in improving electrical signals through chemical/physical interactions with DA molecules [17–18]. Specifically, carbon family nanomaterials [19–23], such as single-walled and multiwalled carbon nanotubes, graphene and its derivatives, and fullerenes, are emerging as an effective strategy to enhance catalytic efficiency towards targets with their outstanding electrical properties. And they are capable of availably enhancing DA-specific responses as well, because they can strongly absorb DA molecules through π-π stacking [11,15,24–25]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are few reports about electrocatalytic sensing application for DA based on graphdiyne (GDY) and its derivatives, an emerging carbon allotrope of growing interest. The exploration primarily focusing on the electrocatalytic mechanism towards DA of GDY-based nanostructures is rarely reported as well.

GDY, a new carbon allotropes, is constituted of aromatic rings of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms that are connected with butadiyne linkers of sp-hybridized carbon atoms, forming a 2D network structure with a one-atomic thick. The characteristic atomic arrangement structure endows GDY quite fascinating properties superior to conventional carbon materials, covering infinite natural pores and band gap, high conductivity, high conjugation, more structural flexibility, affluent bonds and interesting chemical reactivity [26–29]. Certainly, extra modifying and defect designing would be beneficial to further expand their reactive performance for GDY-based materials. GDY is very stable as it has a naturally generating structure with benzene rings linked by acetylenic groups that declares its high stability even at high temperature, which widely expands its application areas [30]. GDY exhibits both intense visible light absorption ability and accelerated charge separation capability due to the existing π-conjugation structure [31]. What’s more, the highly porous plane of GDY offers a high-performance and dispersive network structure that would be beneficial for more anchoring and would provide more sufficient reactive sites that are suitable for diversiform biomolecules, metal ions, and gases. All these valuable characteristics make GDY-based materials very suitable for various potential fields, for example, in batteries [32–34], photocatalysts, electrocatalysts and supporter for catalysts [35–37], photoelectrochemical water splitting [38], supercapacitors [39]. More importantly, in view of their particular conjugation structure, high conductivity, numerous active sites and good biocompatibility, GDY and GDY-based materials have been explored for biological, biomedical and biosensing applications [40–50]. Various composites based on GDY were synthesized and their antibacterial efficiency was investigated and demonstrated [40–42]. In biomedical field, interestingly, graphdiyne oxide (GDYO) nanosheets with its remarkable mechanical, electronic and thermal peculiarities, have been found to be a better alternative for cancer immunotherapy, from which GDYO would invoke innate and adaptive arms of immune system and subsequently enhance cancer immunotherapy efficacy [43]. The introduction of N element in N-GDY nanostructure can create numerous heteroatomic defects and interactive sites, further enhancing its electrochemical properties. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, although so many biological and biomedical application fields of GDY-based materials are explored around the world, very few reports about electrocatalytic sensing for DA with GDY and its derivatives are found, particularly the direct electrocatalytic detection of DA in complex biofluids where a good deal of biological components are existing that would lead to an overwhelming signal suppression for various sensors. To sum up, we are cheerfully looking forward to explore the unprecedented opportunities of GDY-based nanostructures for high-performance electrocatalytic sensing towards multiple biomolecules depended on their inherently conspicuous physical/chemical properties.

Herein, we demonstrated a highly anti-interference, selective and sensitive electrocatalytic sensor for DA detecting with nitrogen-doped GDY (N-GDY), where the electrocatalytic efficacy towards was observably boosted. We synthesized N-GDY, characterized its structure in detailed by Raman, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra, Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Transmission electron microscope (TEM) techniques, respectively, and surveyed its electrocatalytic performance towards DA through the electrochemical techniques. N element introduction can provide more suitable interactive sites for DA molecules. The abundant existing sp-N sites were proved to be the best-matched sites for DA molecules. Both the best-matched sp-N reactive sites and the related electrocatalytic mechanism towards DA were explored, verified and revealed through the density functional theory (DFT) calculations that have been widely applied in many fields [51–54]. The interactions between target and sp-N effectively excluded other component adsorptions that powerfully guaranteed excellent anti-interference ability and further enhanced selectivity of such DA sensor. The large electroactive surface area, more interactive sites, high conductivity, improved interfacial charge transfer of N-GDY boosted sensitivity of such DA sensor. Our designed DA sensor achieved a high-efficiency target recognition with a wide actual performance range from 1.0 µM to 550 µM and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.46 µM. Most importantly, such DA sensor realized the direct DA detection in complex biofluids of artificial sweat and 50% (V/V) fetal bovine serum (FBS), severally. As we all know, a good deal of biological components were co-existing in biofluids that may lead to overwhelming signal interference. The achievements in complex biofluids of DA sensor declared its superior anti-interference ability [55–58].

DA, ethyl alcohol (EA), glucose (GL), lactic acid (LA), glycine (GLY), KCl, fetal bovine serum (FBS) and artificial sweat were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade. The water used was obtained from a Milli-Q system.

SEM and TEM were, respectively, conducted on S-4800 (Hitachi HighTechnology Co., Ltd., Japan) and JSM-2100 systems. XPS was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. Raman spectra was operated with a Lab RAM HR800 assisted with an effective laser power on the as-prepared N-GDY sample of 5 mW, an excitation laser wavelength of 532 nm and a spot size of 1 mm. FTIR spectra were collected with a TENSOR 27 FTIR spectrometer (Scotia, NY, USA) in the range from 4000 to 500 cm−1.

Firstly, GDY was synthesized according to the previous reports [34–36]. The hydrothermal synthesis procedure of N-GDY was illustrated as following: 1) Firstly, 20.0 mg GDY and 40.0 mg melamine were mixed and uniformly dispersed in water (30 mL) for 10 min, afterwards, the mixture solution was heated in Teflon-lined stainless-steal autoclave (150 °C) for 15 h. 2) Next, above mixture solution was dried at freezing vacuum dryer overnight. 3) Finally, the precursor was placed in a tube furnace at 800 °C for 2 h and was kept at Ar flowing of 150 mL/min. N-GDY was synthesized here.

3.0 mg N-GDY was dispersed into an ink consisted of ethanol (0.30 mL) and water (0.15 mL) with the addition of 60.0 μL Nafion (5.0 wt%) with ultrasonic treatment to form an uniform mixed solution. 7 µL homogeneous N-GDY solution was rapidly dropped onto bare GCE surface to produce the electrocatalytic sensing interface. Developed DA sensor was measured in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.2 M, pH 7.4), artificial sweat, and 50% (V/V) FBS, respectively. The fabrication and electrocatalytic procedures were schematically shown in Scheme 1 .

In this work, a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd.) was used to survey the electrochemical measurements. The three-electrode system was consisted of a reference electrode of Ag/AgCl, a working electrode of bare or N-GDY modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE), and a counter electrode of platinum wire. Electrochemical i-t curve was tested in PBS (0.2 M, pH 7.4), artificial sweat and 50% (V/V) FBS under a constant magnetic stirring with an applied oxidation voltage of 0.45 V. Other electrochemical measurements including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were tested in PBS containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl.

XPS was employed to characterize the synthesized N-GDY and GDY nanostructures. XPS spectrum showed that the as-prepared N-GDY and GDY contained the representative C, O element peaks, and an obvious N peak that appeared after the N doping procedure (Fig. 1
a and d). Compared with that of GDY consisted of two graphite N (Grap-N) peaks at 399.3 and 401.4 eV, severally (Fig. 1 e), after the N doping, N 1 s spectrum of N-GDY (Fig. 1 b) can be deconvoluted into four peaks, which represented for the Grap-N at 400.4 eV, sp-N at 397.5 eV, pyridine-N (Pyri-N) at 397.8 eV, and amino N (Amino-N) at 399.1 eV, respectively [59]. All these N 1 s peaks indicated the successful N doping into carbon skeleton of N-GDY nanostructure, which could offer more suitable interaction sites for target molecules. Fig. 1 c exhibited the C 1 s peak of N-GDY that can be merged into five bonds of C-C (sp) at 284.8 eV, C-C (sp2) at 284.4 eV, C = O at 288.3 eV, and C = N at 285.8 eV, C-N at 286.4 eV that distinctly declared the successful N doping procedure. Because before the N doping procedure, the C 1 s peak of GDY was corresponding to four bonds (C-C (sp) at 284.9 eV, C-C (sp2) at 284.3 eV, C-O at 286.5 eV, CO at 288.5 eV) without the bonds of C-N and CN as shown in Fig. 1 f. Fig. 1 g presented the Raman spectroscopy of N-GDY and GDY to verify the N doping procedure. From Fig. 1 g, the two peaks at about 1360 and 1580 cm−1, respectively, correspond to the D and G bands of N-GDY [59]. In contrasted to GDY, the ID/IG values for N-GDY became obviously bigger, indicating the generation of more defects in N-GDY that was resulted from the N doping process. Fig. 1 h revealed the FTIR spectrum of N-GDY, which confirmed the results of XPS and further verified the successful N element doping.

The surface morphology, representative element constitution and distribution characteristics of N-GDY and GDY nanostructures were examined using SEM and TEM (Fig. 2 ). N-GDY surface exhibited a uniformed, creviced and multilayer morphology from Fig. 2 a and b at different enlarged sizes (5 µm, 1 µm, 500 nm and 200 nm), which can provide large electroactive surface area, more efficient electron transport opportunities and more approachable active sites for the interactions of target molecules. GDY surface morphology was also showed in Fig. 2 c and d, from which no obviously morphological difference was observed, indicating the N-doping procedure could not change the aggregation status of GDY. The TEM image (Fig. 2e) and consistently typical element mapping tests (Fig. 2f, g and h) further confirmed the successful preparation of N-doped GDY. Besides C (Fig. 2 f) and O (Fig. 2 g) elements, the typical N element was visibly found (Fig. 2 h) that was the proof of successful doping of N element into GDY. The introduction of N element into GDY plane may provide more interactive sites for DA molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and electrocatalytic behaviors investigation of N-GDY
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical techniques of CV and EIS were used, separately, to investigate the electrochemical and electrocatalytic behaviors of N-GDY modified GCE (N-GDY/GCE) (Fig. 3 ). As we all know, EIS as an effective method is capable of monitoring and tracing exhaustive evidence on interfacial characteristic of modified electrodes. Nyquist spectrum is composed of a semicircular part and a linear portion at higher and lower frequencies, signifying the typical fingerprints of charge transfer and diffusion limited process, separately. In EIS, the semicircle diameter is quantified as the electron-transfer resistance (Rct) of electrode surface. Fig. 3 a and b showed the CV and EIS Bode plots of N-GDY/GCE in PBS, respectively. Although no discernible current peak was observed for both bare GCE and N-GDY/GCE in PBS (Fig. 3 a) because of the absence of redox probes, the current response for N-GDY/GCE (red curve) was dramatically enlarged than that of bare GCE (black curve), evidently declaring the high conductivity of N-GDY/GCE. The high conductivity of N-GDY was also testified by the impedance measurement as shown in Fig. 3 b, from which when N-GDY was covered onto bare electrode surface the impedance became very decreased (red curve) in contrast with bare GCE (black curve). When the electrochemical behaviors were conducted in PBS containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1 M KCl as shown in Fig. 3 c and d, the CV response and redox peaks largely increased and the EIS (semicircle diameter) obviously decreased (red curves in Fig. 3 c and d), respectively, compared with that of bare GCE (black curves in Fig. 3 c and d), further verifying the excellent conductivity and large electroactive surface area of N-GDY electrode that decreased the interfacial resistance and encouraged the electron-transfer between electrode surface and electrolyte.

The electrocatalytic sensing performance towards DA of N-GDY/GCE was surveyed subsequently (Fig. 3 e and f) using electrochemical CV and DPV methods. For the CV response of bare GCE in PBS, no redox peak was observed from Fig. 3 e (black curve), but after 1.0 mM DA was added, a pair of obvious redox peaks appeared (red curve) that indicated a certain of electrocatalytic property of GCE towards DA. In stark contrast, for N-GDY/GCE (Fig. 3 e), a pair of more remarkable catalytic redox peaks could be observed in 1.0 mM DA solution (green curve) contrasted with the signal only in PBS (blue curve), and of course, a stronger current response was found as well for N-GDY electrode. Fig. 3 f displayed the DPV signals of N-GDY/GCE and bare GCE towards DA, from which the bigger electrochemical response and stronger electrocatalytic ability towards DA were obtained by the introduction of N-GDY substrate (blue and green curves) onto bare GCE surface (black and red curves). All these DPV signals were fairly consistent with the CV responses. Evidently, all these results illustrated the excellent electrochemical and eletrocatalytic capabilities of N-GDY, depending on its large electroactive surface area, more active sites, high electrical conductivity and low interfacial impedance properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic mechanism exploration of N-GDY towards DA
ce:section.ce:para:
Hence, to powerfully reveal the eletrocatalytic process, we performed DFT calculations for the first time, to the best of our knowledge, to explore both the best-matched interactive sites on N-GDY surface for target DA molecules and the related electrocatalytic mechanism in detailed [60–61]. We firstly constructed a unit cell (2 × 2) to represent the single layer of the pure GDY. According to the classes of C-N bonding configurations, four kinds of N-substituted GDY including N1-GDY (sp-N), N2-GDY (sp-N), Pyri-N-GDY and Grap-N-GDY, respectively, which were coincident with the XPS characterization, were considered in the present work as shown in Fig. 4
a-d. The geometries were optimized using PBC model implemented in the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) [62–63]. The projector augmented wave pseudopotentials were employed to represent the interaction between the core ions and the valence electrons [64]. Meanwhile, the exchange–correlation effects were mainly described by the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof generalized-gradient approximation with a plane-wave basis cutoff of 500 eV [64–65]. A 3 × 3 × 1 Monkhurst-Pack reciprocal space grid is employed with a vacuum layer of 20 Å. All calculations used the DFT-D3 [66] method to account for van der Waals interactions, set 0.01 eV/Å as the force convergence criterion and 10-5 eV as the electron self-consistent iteration energy. After optimizing the geometries of the four different kinds of N-GDYs, the systems of DA2- adsorbed on the monolayer were also optimized and the adsorption energy (Eads) of DA2- on N-GDY monolayer was computed as:

where E tot, E ads, and E N-GDY represent the energy of the total system, the isolated DA2-, and N-GDY monolayer, respectively.

The Bader effective charges of selected atoms were labeled in Fig. 4 a-d, where the negative value represented to obtain charge and positive value represented to loss charge. From Fig. 4 a-d, we found that the N doping procedure introduced the electronic redistribution in N-GDY nanostructure, leading to enhanced positive charge of some C atoms that was beneficial for DA2- adsorption. As we know, DA molecule can manifest a co-existing balance between phenoxy anion and H+ in PBS, and phenoxy anion can be adsorbed onto developed N-GDY surface, preferentially the introduced N sites, because of the producing of a stable donor-bridge-acceptor complex structure [59–61]. The doped N and the adjacent C atoms exhibited larger net charges compared to the other sites of GDY, favoring the adsorption of DA2-. The adsorption of DA2- on N1-GDY (sp-N), N2-GDY (sp-N), Pyri-N-GDY and Grap-N-GDY are found to be 2.755, 3.495, 3.335 and 4.230 eV, indicating the endothermic process. We also observed the DA/N1-GDY showed the smallest adsorption energy, indicating it was the most possible configuration among these four different adsorption sites. The produced complex structures between DA2- and four different N sites were presented in Fig. 4 e-h. Fig. 4 e showed the most stable complex structure after DA molecule was adsorbed onto the best-matched N1-GDY (sp-N) site, which was accompanied by the generation of electrochemical signal during electrocatalytic process, further revealing the related catalytic mechanism of N-GDY towards DA.

It is noted that the stability of the four kinds of N-substituted GDY have been tested by the previous works [67–70]. Here we also performed the frequency analysis for the structures of DA2- adsorbed on the four kinds of N-substituted GDY. We found there is no imaginary frequency for the structure of DA2-/N2-GDY and DA2-/Pyri-N-GDY, while an imaginary frequency of 8.766 cm−1 and 44.052 cm−1 appears for the structure of DA2-/N1-GYD and DA2-/Grap-N-GDY, respectively. The very small imaginary frequencies indicates the high stability of the complex structures of DA2- adsorbed on the four kinds of N-substituted GDY.

In summary, through the DFT calculations, firstly, the N doping procedure could lead to an electronic redistribution in N-GDY compared with the highly positive charged GDY. Next, the most approachable interactive sites were the N1-GDY (sp-N) on N-GDY surface, because this interaction process between N1-GDY (sp-N) and DA molecule showed the smallest adsorption energy in contrasted with other N sites, indicating of the most possible configuration and the most stable structure. Thirdly, the complex structures of DA2- adsorbed on the four kinds of N-substituted GDY showed high stability as well. Finally, the efficient combination generated a more stable complex structure (Fig. 4 e) that can effectively exclude other biological component adsorptions on such N-GDY substrate surface to further enhance the analytical selectivity towards target DA [59–60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of electrocatalytic DA sensing conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To realize an optimum electrocatalytic sensing of DA, we used the electrochemical i-t curve technique to inquiry the effect of different applied potential on such fabricated N-GDY/GCE ( Fig. S1 ). The applied potential was surveyed with i-t through a successive detection of 100.0 μM DA. From Fig. S1 , the fabricated N-GDY/GCE obtained the biggest current response towards DA with an acceptable background noise when the applied potential was set at 0.45 V. Thus, in this N-GDY-based electrocatalytic process, 0.45 V was used as the optimal applied potential for all the subsequent amperometric measurements. Furthermore, the effect of different pH values of electrolyte was also evaluated using DPV measurement ( Fig. S2 ). From Fig. S2 , the DPV peak potential shifts negligibly and the DPV responses kept very stable at different pH values. The result manifested that the DA sensor was insensitive to pH variation, so it was fully suitable for DA analysis in real complex media.

The specific electroactive surface area of the obtained N-GDY modified GCE was measured ( Supporting Information ) using CV monitoring in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.1 M KCl at a series of scan rate ( Fig. S3 ) [71–72]. Through the CV tests, the N-GDY decorated electrode presented a 5.9 times larger area than that of bare GCE, which can provide a very large exposed surface area and abundant accessible reactive sites for the interactions of DA molecules [71–72]. Therefore, the electrocatalytic sensing of DA with N-GDY/GCE was measured. The amperometric response of such N-GDY/GCE electrocatalytic sensor towards DA was tested with an applied oxidation voltage of 0.45 V, accompanied by the continuously adding of a gradient of target DA in PBS under a constant magnetic stirring (Fig. 5
a). The corresponding calibration plot was exhibited as Fig. 5 b, from which a wide linear range from 1 to 550 μM was found with a high sensitivity of 0.374 µA µM−1 cm−2. The limit of detection (LOD) of such developed electrocatalytic sensor towards DA was calculated to be 0.46 µM (S/N = 3). In general, such electrocatalytic DA sensor exhibited a low LOD and a wide linear concentration range, principally attributing to the large electroactive surface area, more approachable reactive sites, enhanced conductivity and improved interfacial electron transfer of as-synthesized N-GDY substrate.

Straight after, we have explored the selectivity of such DA electrocatalytic sensing platform. Fig. 5 c showed the amperometric measurements of several interfering components conducted with the adding of lower and higher concentrations of DA (20 and 100 µM), respectively, and some greater levels of interfering substances such as 50 µM of EA, 100 µM of GL, 1 mM of LA, GLY and KCl in PBS sequentially. The sensing platform only exhibited significant current response towards DA and others showed a negligible influence on DA determination, which manifested its excellent selectivity. Besides, to further survey the selectivity, norepinephrine and epinephrine were also tested with developed DA sensor as shown in Fig. S4 , from which no obvious signal enlargement was observed. The outstanding selectivity was primarily ascribed to the high-efficiency interactions between DA molecules and sp-N active sites on N-GDY surface that availably excluded other interference adsorptions and further led to a high selectivity of sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analyses of DA in real complex biofluids
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the practical application feasibility and capability of such DA sensor, target DA determinations were conducted in real complex biofluids of artificial sweat and FBS, where a good deal of biological components are co-existing that may cause an overwhelming signal suppression if the developed sensors are not adequately selective and sensitive. Under the optimal applied oxidation voltage, the amperometric responses of as-prepared N-GDY/GCE electrocatalytic sensors towards target DA were measured in undiluted artificial sweat and 50% (V/V) FBS diluted with PBS, respectively, with the continuously adding of a gradient concentration of DA under a constant magnetic stirring (Fig. 6
a and c). Fig. 6 b and d exhibited the corresponding calibration plots, from which the same linear range from 1 to 550 μM in artificial sweat (Fig. 6 b) and 50% FBS (Fig. 6 d), severally, as that of in PBS, have been found, indicating no obvious signal suppression generating. These consistent results manifested excellent anti-interference ability, high selectivity and sensitivity of such developed DA sensor which was capable of operating in complex biofluids with satisfying performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the DA sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Reproducibility and stability are two vital parameters affecting performance of sensors. We proceeded 13 successive measurements towards 100 µm DA using one same constructed sensor with the applied potential of 0.45 V in PBS under a constant magnetic stirring, as shown in Fig. 7
a, from which the response decreased after 11 measurements, declaring a good reusability and stability of such DA sensor. The electrode-to-electrode reproducibility was also measured using 5 independently prepared N-GDY/GCE electrodes to detect a same level of DA target (Fig. 7 b). The considerable consistent results indicated an excellent reproducibility of the DA sensor. The stability of N-GDY/GCE electrode was tested through a continuous CV scans as shown in Fig. 7 c, and no obvious current decrease was observed even an up to 2000 scan cycle, directly claiming the excellent electrochemical stability of electrocatalytic DA sensor. Furthermore, the modified electrodes after testing were washed with water and PBS adequately, and then they were kept in PBS across 15 days to evaluate the long-term storage stability of such DA sensor (Fig. 7 d). The sensors still retained more than 95% of initial responses to the analyte DA, demonstrating their good long-period storage stability.

The sensing performance comparison of many electrochemical sensors was shown in Table S1 . Although some lower LODs could be found from other reports [56,59,73,74,75], our fabricated sensor manifested both a wide performance range from 1 to 550 µM than others and a powerful practical application ability of direct target analyses in real complex biofluids, because other studies realized DA detections only by spiking and recovering method. Our detection results definitely demonstrated its high anti-interference ability and promisingly clinical application potential of such DA sensor. The reasons could be summarized: 1) the high-efficiency interactions between sp-N active sites and DA molecules effectively excluded other component combinations in biofluids and enhanced the selectivity, 2) the superior conductivity and improved electron transfer of N-GDY ensured the sensitivity. The GDY-based nanostructures can be applied in numerous other catalytic processes of pharmaceutical, biomedical and agricultural interests [57–58], where the catalyzing performance would be significantly boosted.

The anti-interference ability, electrocatalytic selectivity and sensitivity towards DA of the fabricated electrocatalytic sensor can be significantly boosted depended on as-synthesized N-doped GDY nanostructure. The best-matched interactive sites for DA molecules on N-GDY surface were the abundant existing sp-N sites. Both the sp-N active sites for DA molecules and the relevant electrocatalytic mechanism towards DA of N-GDY sensor were explored, verified and revealed by the DFT calculations for the first time to the best of our knowledge. The interactions between sp-N sites and DA molecules effectively excluded other component adsorptions with sensing interface, thus, ensuring high anti-interference ability and further enhancing selectivity of such DA sensor. The as-synthesized N-GDY nanostructure offered large electroactive surface area, more reactive sites, superior conductivity and interfacial charge transfer for the electrocatalytic process, which significantly increased sensitivity of fabricated sensor. Most importantly, such N-GDY sensor achieved the quantitative analyses of target DA with high selectivity and sensitivity even in real complex biofluids, where a good deal of biological components were co-existing, primarily attributing to the excellent anti-interference ability. The electrocatalytic application of N-GDY would be extended to numerous other catalytic processes including biomedical, pharmaceutical and agricultural interests, to further provide theoretical foundation and technical assistance for human health and food safety.
